| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | For Velorian ModuleShield-584, record whether the busbar-insulator review follows an electrical event, dropped-tool incident, or routine service access, and identify the exact module section carrying the suspect shield. Photograph the shield edge and the exposed busbar geometry before anything is shifted. |
| T2 | Expose the busbar shield, retaining tabs, busbar edges, stud exits, foam barriers, and cover ribs without moving the shield from its as-found seat. The shield edge and the underlying busbar have to be judged together because an intact shield can still be unsafe if it is mis-seated. |
| T3 | Inspect the shield for cracks, heat spots, missing tabs, rub marks, trimmed openings, or loss of stand-off at the busbar edge and stud shoulder. Record which retaining feature or edge actually lost coverage. |
| T4 | Compare the suspect shield position with the neighboring shield or with the untouched side of the same module so tab engagement, stand-off, and busbar coverage are judged against a real reference. This prevents a cosmetic acceptance of a misaligned but unbroken part. |
| T5 | Inspect the neighboring foam barriers and cover ribs for contact witness that could push the shield sideways during closure, then restore only the failed tab, shield panel, or interfering support feature. The coverage path from retaining tab to busbar edge has to remain explicit. |
| T6 | Refit surrounding parts and confirm that the shield still maintains the required stand-off to the busbar edges and stud exits after final loading. A shield that sits correctly only before the cover rib is installed is not acceptable. |
| T7 | End the insulator review only when the note names the exact concern, such as cracked shield panel, missing retaining tab, mis-seated shield edge, or cover-rib interference, and ties it to the final coverage result. The release boundary is a shield that fully covers the intended busbar edges and holds position after the neighboring foam and cover features are restored. |

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | For Novaris Busbar-583, record whether the contamination was found after service, coolant leak, or foreign-object entry, and identify the exact busbar span before cleaning begins. Save the stain pattern and the neighboring shield position before disturbing the area. |
| T2 | Expose the affected busbar surface, insulator cover, fastener zone, and neighboring hardware while preserving the contaminant pattern so the origin can still be inferred after inspection. Keep the busbar face and the cover edge in the same view. |
| T3 | Inspect whether the material is coolant residue, metal debris, carbonized dust, or assembly paste, because each contaminant type carries different electrical and corrosion implications. Record whether the contamination remains on the accessible face or reaches the clamp interface. |
| T4 | Confirm that the contamination has not migrated into the terminal joint, insulator cavity, or fastener stack where surface cleaning alone would be insufficient, then compare the affected span with a clean neighboring busbar section. This ties the risk to one named span or joint. |
| T5 | Clean the approved surface and inspect for staining, plating damage, or heat marks left behind, then verify that the insulator shield still covers the cleaned busbar correctly and that no debris remains trapped at the shield edge. The cleaned surface and the restored cover geometry have to agree. |
| T6 | Reinspect the busbar surface and shield after final loading because a cover or fastener that shifts slightly can rub or recontaminate the cleaned span once the neighboring hardware is back in place. This keeps the acceptance on the true assembled state. |
| T7 | Close the busbar-surface review only when the note names the contaminated span, the contaminant type, the joint or cavity status beneath it, and the final shield coverage at that location. The release boundary is a clean busbar surface with no unresolved contamination at the clamp interface and with the insulator cover seated correctly after reassembly. |

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | For Aurex ModulePad-495, record which compression-pad section is under audit and whether the concern is missing pad, shifted pad, folded edge, or uneven witness after module service, then identify the exact module interface. Save the original pad witness before it is touched. |
| T2 | Expose the pad location, module face, compression member, and nearby harness or vent hardware without pulling the pad away before its original position is documented. Keep the pad edge and the compression member visible together because both define the witness. |
| T3 | Inspect pad coverage, thickness, compression witness, and edge alignment so the audit distinguishes a correctly compressed pad from a pad that has migrated or folded under load. Record whether the witness is lost at one edge, one corner, or across the full section. |
| T4 | Compare the suspect pad with the matching pad on the opposite module side or neighboring module so acceptable overlap and compression are judged against a true reference. This keeps the correction on one specific pad section. |
| T5 | Correct the pad position or replace the damaged pad section, then close the compression member and confirm that the pad does not slide, fold, or trap adjacent harness or vent hardware as load builds. The corrected witness must remain stable through closure. |
| T6 | Reinspect the pad witness after the module stack has settled because some shifted or memory-set pads reopen a weak edge only after the compression member relaxes. This final reread ties the result to the actual loaded condition. |
| T7 | Close the pad audit only when the note names the pad location, observed issue, any replacement or repositioning performed, and the final accepted overlap and witness at that interface. The release boundary is a compression pad that covers the intended module face and holds its witness without renewed shift after settling. |

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | On Kestrel VMC-850, record the spindle chiller complaint as coolant dripping from the rear column only during circulation, residue collecting on the lower cable tray, or coolant loss that appears after a sustained 8000 to 12000 rpm spindle hold. Note the as-received coolant level, spindle speed window, and the first visible drip path before any guard is removed. |
| T2 | Isolate the machine as required and expose spindle-chiller supply hose HC-S1, return hose HC-R1, worm-drive clamps CL-S1 and CL-R1, brass tube necks, chiller manifold block, P-clip PC-RC1 on the rear column, and the lower drip tray. Keep the upper outlet hose and lower return hose visible together so upstream and downstream wetting can be separated instead of being read as one large stain. |
| T3 | Trace the wet boundary from the lowest drip point back toward the upper hose bead, the clamp screw housing, the molded hose bend, or the manifold face, and document whether the stain runs front to rear along the cable tray or drops straight down from the hose neck. Preserve hose twist and clip position because a side-loaded branch often leaks only when the machine is in its final supported geometry. |
| T4 | Pressure-test the branch at 1.5 bar with the spindle stopped and compare the result with the dry reference hose on the opposite side of the chiller block. If branch HC-R1 stays dry under static pressure but wets the lower tray once the chiller pump runs, keep the evidence on vibration load, clamp position, and hose insertion depth rather than replacing the manifold on assumption. |
| T5 | Measure clamp position relative to the tube bead and check hose insertion depth at both the upper outlet neck and the lower return neck. A clamp that sits more than a few millimeters behind the bead or a hose that is pulled rearward by the P-clip can look seated while still opening a leak path once pump pulsation and spindle vibration act on the branch together. |
| T6 | Run the chiller pump and hold the spindle at the complaint speed with the rear guard still open, then watch the upper clamp, the molded bend, and the P-clip bracket through the full vibration event. If wetting starts only when the hose is tugged toward the rear column clip, isolate the fault boundary to the upper return-hose clamp and clip-to-bracket side load rather than to the manifold casting or the lower drain path. |
| T7 | Reposition the hose fully against the tube bead, rotate the clamp so the screw housing does not contact the column bracket, and restore the branch through the original clip path with a relaxed bend radius. Confirm that the hose is seated against the bead, the clamp band is centered, and the P-clip is holding the branch without twisting the upper neck sideways. |
| T8 | Release the machine only after a repeated pressure hold and a repeated high-speed circulation run leave the upper hose neck, lower return path, and drip tray dry. The accepted closeout is a named leak boundary at return-hose clamp CL-R1 or support path PC-RC1, with no renewed coolant trace from upper outlet to lower tray under the same speed window that created the complaint. |

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | On Arcline VTC-630, record the complaint as tool pull-out during face milling, weak unclamp feel, or drawbar alarm on a CAT40 spindle, and note the toolholder style, cutting load, and whether the issue followed recent drawbar service. The intake note must already tie the problem to the drawbar clamp stack and not to a generic spindle fault. |
| T2 | Warm the spindle to the service temperature if required, then expose drawbar booster cylinder CY-DB1, Belleville stack housing BS-DB1, unclamp valve SV-UC1, pressure transducer port PT-UC1, and the access path to the spindle pull-force test point. Keep the upper actuator side and lower retention side visible in the same inspection window so stroke and clamp force can be judged against the same hardware state. |
| T3 | Install the calibrated clamp-force gauge in a verified CAT40 holder, run the clamp cycle, and record the measured pull force rather than relying on sound or feel. Compare the cold and warmed values if the complaint changes with temperature, because a weak Belleville stack and a short hydraulic stroke do not deteriorate in the same way. |
| T4 | Measure unclamp pressure at booster cylinder CY-DB1 through port PT-UC1 and compare it with the machine target while watching drawbar movement. | Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | On Novaris VMC-810, record the warm-up complaint as Y-axis vibration or chatter during reversal between 200 mm and 240 mm from home after approximately fifteen minutes of cycling, and note the feed rate, payload, and coolant state. This file is tied to the Y-axis ball-screw and rear support hardware, not to the spindle or the machine generally. |
| T2 | Run the same Y-axis motion window that produced the complaint and observe rear fixed bearing block BR-YR1, ball-screw shaft, nut housing, rear linear-guide trucks, way-cover segment, and lube manifold while the machine warms. Keep rear support and moving nut housing visible together so the vibration can be tied to one motion boundary instead of to background machine noise. |
| T3 | Measure vibration at rear bearing block BR-YR1 and compare it with the value at mid-travel or on X-axis reference condition, then note screw-surface temperature at sensor TS-YS when the complaint appears. A vibration peak that grows only after the rear support warms points to preload or support loss, while a constant vibration at all temperatures points elsewhere. |
| T4 | Inspect the rear locknut, bearing cap, way-cover leaf, guide-truck lubrication marks, and the screw-to-cover clearance for evidence of fretting, paint shift, dry track, or cover rub. Compare upper and lower way-cover contact marks so a loose support bearing is not confused with a cover leaf that is only grazing the screw near reversal. |
| T5 | Measure axial float or backlash at the Y-axis screw while the machine remains warm, then compare that value with the cold or accepted reference. If axial float at the rear support exceeds the expected band and vibration drops when the support is preloaded by hand, isolate the fault boundary to the rear fixed-bearing locknut or bearing-preload stack rather than to the nut housing or servo tuning. |
| T6 | Restore rear bearing preload, lock the nut, and verify that the support block sits squarely against the saddle casting with the lube line and way-cover segment returned to their original paths. Confirm that the cover leaf no longer touches the screw and that the rear guide trucks remain lubricated rather than dry at the same travel zone. |
| T7 | Repeat the warm-up cycle through the same feed range and reversal points, then compare hot vibration, screw temperature, and backlash with the pre-repair values. The corrected condition must hold through the actual motion window that created the complaint, not just during a short cold jog near home. |
| T8 | Inspect the rear bearing block, way-cover leaf, lube manifold fittings, and guide-truck area again after the repeated motion cycle because settling after travel often reveals a loose fastener or displaced cover segment. This second inspection confirms that the restored preload did not simply shift the symptom to an adjacent support part. |
| T9 | Close the work only after the record names the exact boundary, such as rear fixed-bearing preload at BR-YR1, locknut movement, cover interference, guide-truck lubrication loss, or no defect found at the checked support, and ties it to measured vibration and axial float. Accept the repair when Y-axis reversal remains smooth through the original warm motion window and the rear support hardware holds its corrected position. |

| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | Define the leaking branch on Helion PackLine-327 EV as the battery coolant quick connector at either the inlet or outlet side, and note whether coolant loss is active, dried, pressure-dependent, or only found after the vehicle sits overnight. Keep the opening complaint tied to the exact connector position and leak behavior so later pressure checks stay on the same repaired branch. |
| T2 | Isolate the pack, drain only the affected local cooling branch, and remove the side shield so the quick connector, retaining clip, male tube, O-ring land, hose end, and nearby support bracket are fully exposed for replacement. Keep the connector, male tube, and bracket geometry visible together because hose side-load often returns only when the support hardware is restored. |
| T3 | Release the retaining clip in a controlled way, disconnect the quick connector, and inspect the latch windows, inner O-ring seat, male tube surface, insertion stop, and hose-end condition for scoring, flattening, cracked plastic, or remnants of the old seal. This first read needs to distinguish seal damage from tube scoring before new parts are fitted and the original witness pattern is lost. |
| T4 | Clean the male tube and confirm that no old O-ring fragment, dried coolant crystal, burr, or contamination remains on the sealing land, because even a small scratch or trapped debris can cut the new seal during reassembly. The sealing land has to remain clean and continuous from insertion stop to O-ring track before the replacement connector is pushed home. |
| T5 | Install the replacement quick connector with the correct O-ring orientation, confirm full tube insertion against the stop, and verify that the retaining clip sits flush and symmetric in both lock windows instead of riding one side of the connector body. |
| T6 | Restore the hose support and bracket position so the new connector is not held in side load, checking hose twist, clamp clocking, bracket alignment, and the natural free angle of the hose once the support hardware is returned. The connector should leave the bracket and hose span in a neutral installed state, not in a forced alignment that will reopen the leak after circulation begins. |
| T7 | Refill and bleed the affected cooling branch, watching the reservoir response, bleed return, and the new quick connector for trapped air release, fluid pulsing, or fresh wetting during the first circulation cycle. Record whether the first visible wetting belongs to trapped-air purge or to a true seal failure at the new connector body or O-ring seat. |
| T8 | Hold pressure on the repaired branch and confirm that the new connector body, O-ring seat, retaining clip, hose orientation, and bracket support remain dry under both static hold and pump-induced flow. This pressure hold has to prove both sealing and neutral support geometry after the branch settles back into its normal loaded route. |
| T9 | Restore all access parts and close the job only after the replacement record includes the exact connector location, removed failure mode, new connector seating confirmation, bleed result, and final dry-check outcome. The closeout should identify the exact replaced connector and the final dry result in the same repaired branch so the next service event starts from one named hardware boundary. |

|---|---|
| T1 | Start the enclosure-seal replacement on Arcline Logic-579 LR by reviewing the moisture complaint or prior leak history and checking the BMS enclosure perimeter first, so the new seal is not installed over a lid, flange, or pass-through defect that would immediately recreate the same leak path. Record which side or corner showed water tracks, because an upper left pass-through leak and a lower flange seep have different boundaries even though both wet the same enclosure. |
| T2 | Depower the controller, remove the BMS lid, and extract the old seal while tracking the original joint position, flattened sections, adhesive residue, and any corner where the gasket appears pinched, under-compressed, or damaged by previous service work. Keep the removed seal indexed so the final failure can still be tied to one joint, one bolt-boss transition, or one connector pass-through corner after the groove has been cleaned. |
| T3 | Clean the full seal groove, bolt-boss transitions, connector pass-through corners, and lid flange, then inspect for scratches, flange distortion, trapped dirt, previous seal scrap, or burrs; dry-fit the lid if needed to confirm flatness, dowel alignment, and even flange contact before the new gasket goes in. This preparation step should separate a bad gasket from a damaged sealing surface, because a rolled flange or raised burr will defeat any replacement seal installed over it. |
| T4 | Install the replacement enclosure seal with the joint placed away from the known water path, confirming that the gasket sits fully in the groove along straight runs and remains untwisted around the corners, bosses, and pass-through transitions where compression normally varies. Check that the seal height stays consistent around the connector opening and dowel points so no local high spot forces the lid to bridge over one section of the enclosure. |
| T5 | Refit the lid and tighten the fasteners in the intended cross pattern, watching the seal-compression line, lid-to-base gap, and pass-through corners so no one side closes early while another remains visibly high, then perform the specified splash or low-pressure leak confirmation on the enclosure boundary. The release check has to prove both uniform compression and a dry enclosure path at the original leak side rather than only a visually closed lid. |
| T6 | Release the controller area only after the work order includes the seal lot, joint location, flange condition, dowel alignment result, tightening sequence, pass-through check, original leak side, and final closure result for the BMS enclosure seal replacement. The accepted boundary is an enclosure that closes evenly, keeps the new gasket seated through every corner transition, and shows no renewed moisture path at the previously identified leak zone. |

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | Define the drive-motor coolant-hose leak on Helion DriveUnit-586 Plus by capturing whether it is active hot, active cold, pressure-dependent, or only visible after soak, and by noting the as-found condition of the motor-side hose routing, clamp position, and surrounding residue field before cleaning begins. |
| T2 | Isolate the traction system, stabilize the local cooling branch, and remove only the shields needed to expose the drive-motor coolant hose, hose clamp, tube neck, support bracket, and adjacent wiring without changing the hose twist from its as-found condition. |
| T3 | Clean only the loose surface residue and trace the wet edge from the hose neck outward, distinguishing seepage at the clamp line, coolant tracking along the hose braid, wetting at the tube bead, or migration from a higher branch above the drive unit. |
| T4 | Apply pressure to the local cooling branch and hold it long enough to see whether fresh wetting starts at the clamp gap, the hose-to-neck interface, the molded hose body, or the bracket-supported bend where the hose is forced off-axis. |
| T5 | Release the clamp only as far as required to inspect the band imprint, hose inner seat, and tube-bead contact area, checking for debris, inner-liner damage, corrosion at the bead, or a double imprint that would explain a repeat leak even with correct external clamp appearance. |
| T6 | Compare the suspect hose joint with the opposite or adjacent cooling branch, checking clamp clocking, insertion depth to the bead, hose twist, bracket alignment, and any polished witness mark showing that the hose was rubbing or being pulled in service. |
| T7 | Inspect the hose clamp, tube bead, hose inner seat, support bracket, and nearby sleeve for deformation, corrosion, clamp overtravel, or misposition that would separate a true leak joint from a routing-induced strain condition. |
| T8 | Relax the bracket side load if required and repeat the pressure check with the hose returned to its natural angle so the leak decision reflects the true joint condition and not an artificially loaded support geometry. |
| T9 | Record the exact leak boundary as hose clamp line, hose body, tube bead interface, or bracket-induced side load, and make sure the final note explains how the pressure test and the as-found residue pattern support that conclusion. |
| T10 | Restore only the protection needed for safe closure and leave the identified hose fault path visible and documented for the next repair action instead of hiding it under a broad coolant-hose leak label. |
